data.title: International Seaways: Strategic Vessel Management And Fundamental Stability Amid Oil Market Volatility
data.description:
It’s been six months since my first analysis of International Seaways, Inc. (INSW). Its value has already risen by over 20%. I understand the market stance despite macroeconomic volatility and geopolitical uncertainty across the globe. But that makes me regret not taking a position on top of some realizations about its valuation. Its topline performance and fundamentals are still robust, which guarantees its strong positioning. Technicals warrant some caution but also show new buying opportunities.

Challenges

Macroeconomic volatility across the globe remains a primary concern for players in the oil and gas industry. For instance, inflation is already at its 14-month high of 3.0%, which means that the YoY price increase has reaccelerated in recent months. This can increase the cost pressures of many businesses, and International Seaways, Inc. is not an exception.

Oil price volatility is another important consideration. While they don’t directly or immediately impact oil tankers, they can influence charter rates and spot rates. Currently, Brent costs $62.89 per barrel, while WTI is $59.44 per barrel. With that, these two oil price benchmarks are very close to their 12-month lows.

This can be a double-edged sword for the company. Why? Lower oil prices can lower the profitability of exploring and producing crude oil. This can impact the demand for oil transportation and tanker rates at some levels. Because INSW has a huge exposure to the spot market, it is also susceptible to volatility risks. As you can see, the spot rate for its Suezmax tankers dropped from $38,044 to $33,310. Meanwhile, the total spot revenue days decreased by 17 days. Its Panamax and MR had lower spot rates and revenue days.

The consolation is that lower oil prices can help INSW lower its fuel expenses. After all, lower crude oil prices also lower the prices of refined products like fuel and gasoline. This was what happened in its most recent performance, considering the decrease in voyage and vessel expenses.

INSW Handles Them Well

Despite these challenges, I believe that INSW is still doing quite well. Although its performance softened last quarter, its strategic operations remained evident. In Q3 2025, its total shipping revenues amounted to $196.4M, down by 12.8% YoY from $225.2M. Its pool revenues remained the primary contributor as they comprised over 70% of the total amount and fell by 14.1% YoY. This should not be surprising considering its high exposure to the spot market. This was slightly offset by time charter revenues, which rose by 6.0% YoY.

INSW offsets the risks by balancing its spot exposure with fixed time charters. This move insulates its operations from market volatility. As you can see, the fixed rates of its crude tankers and product carriers strengthened on a YoY basis despite the lower number of revenue days. So, it’s not surprising that its time and bareboat charter revenues remained resilient. As of October 30, a larger portion of its vessels are subject to fixed rates whether spot or time charter. So, revenues are already locked in regardless of softer market changes this quarter.

Another thing I like about INSW is its well-diversified business model, which offers it some pricing flexibility and margin stability. We know that most of its revenues come from charter hire or its spot market earnings and time charters. But it has specialized services, such as ship-to-ship services. This transfers crude between smaller and larger vessels, especially in parts without or with limited deepwater ports. This is a high-margin revenue stream, so it generates revenues at a manageable cost.

In addition, INSW keeps improving its efficiency through its prudent fleet management. It sells older ships and invests in newer, more fuel-efficient ones like dual-fuel-ready ships. It also invests in line with environmental or IMO standards to ensure its competitiveness in the long run.

This helps it reduce fuel expenses on top of the lower fuel prices. Aside from a more efficient fuel usage, new vessels require less repair and maintenance. As a result, its operating expenses decreased by 5.2%. More interestingly, the percentage of the direct operating expenses decreased to 65.3% versus 67.6%. This means that INSW became less cost-sensitive, which is quite impressive amid stubborn inflation. It was not enough to completely offset the decrease in revenue. But it helped INSW maintain a double-digit operating margin of 40.1%. With that, we can prove that it maintains a well-diversified, high-margin business model.

Another strong point of the company is its robust liquidity. Its operating cash flow remains high enough to cover the whole amount of capex.

INSW complements it with prudent debt management to ensure liquidity. So, it’s not surprising that its cash and cash equivalents remain high and cover the majority of the total assets. In addition, its maturity profile remains well-balanced because over 60% of the total amount will not mature within a year. To be more specific, this portion has a maturity of five years and above. Also, cash and cash equivalents can cover the whole maturing amount of $282.5M in a single payment. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of only 1.09x, which means that INSW only needs a year to repay the whole borrowings at once even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, INWS maintains a huge capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

On a broader view, I believe that INSW will benefit from the interest rate cut by the Fed. Why? Lower interest rates may also lower the borrowing cost of its variable-rate borrowings. This can help improve its profitability and liquidity. In addition, injecting liquidity may boost economic activity to support consumption and production. As a result, demand for energy commodities may increase. Oil tankers may receive the positive spillovers through a potential increase in spot rates.

Valuation

After revisiting its valuation, I had some realizations about INSW. It’s true that INSW trades at higher price multiples using sales and book value relative to the five-year average. However, the market does not seem to care much about whether the stock is cheaper or more expensive than in previous years. The thing is, the stock price will increase as long as the key financial metrics of the company increase.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I decided to use the Dividend Discount Model. For simplicity, I focused on regular dividends and excluded special payouts. With that, I estimated a dividend growth rate of 10.41%. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 7.0%, which is higher than the standard of 5%. That way, I will price in more risks and have a conservative estimation. For its Beta, I simply set it at 1.00 to be consistent with the slope or price direction of the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, I derived a TP or target price of $60.76. If I add a 10% risk consideration, the TP will be $54.68. Hence, its rally is justified and still shows some upside potential.

Technicals

INSW’s technicals are still bullish as the price moves with higher highs and lows above the 200-day SMA. But it warrants some caution as it flattens after the recent downtrend. Note that it’s not below the 50-day SMA, which shows that the current price momentum has weakened relative to the average in the past 50 days. Nonetheless, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which suggests that market sentiments are still optimistic and there is a possibility of a rebound. After all, the flattening of the price trend after the dip shows that selling volume has started to weaken.

Its MACD and RSI can explain it. The MACD line is still trending below the signal line and remains within negative territory along with the histogram. This means that selling volume has outpaced buying volume recently. But the histogram is starting to increase again, which implies that buying volume is also increasing. This can also indicate that buyers found a new support level after the dip. Meanwhile, RSI shows that the dip was prompted by overbuying and profit-taking. Now, the stock is nearly oversold, so buyers may be starting to enter and/or re-enter.

Conclusion

International Seaways, Inc. is not fully insulated from inflationary headwinds and oil market volatility. But its strategic fleet management, well-balanced exposure to spot and time charter rates, well-diversified service offerings, and robust liquidity guarantee sustainability and dividend longevity. Valuation is also realistic, although it’s no longer as attractive as it was in my previous coverage. Technicals are still bullish and show new buying opportunities after the recent dip. I’m upgrading my rating from hold to buy

data.title: Tracking John Rogers' Ariel Investments Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Ariel Investments’ 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Ariel Investments’ regulatory 13F Form filed on 11/14/2025. John Rogers’ 13F portfolio value increased from $8.82B to $9.35B this quarter. The portfolio is diversified with recent 13F reports showing around 150 positions. There are 38 securities that are significantly large (more than ~1% of the portfolio each) and they are the focus of this article. The largest five stakes are at ~17% of the portfolio. Please visit our Tracking John Rogers’ Ariel Investments Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for the fund’s moves during Q2 2025.

Their flagship mutual fund is the Ariel Fund (ARGFX) incepted in 1986. Ariel Fund has a lifetime annualized return of 10.77% compared to 10.65% for the Russell 2500 Index and 11.24% for the S&P 500 Index. The other mutual funds in the group are Ariel Appreciation Fund (CAAPX), Ariel Focus Fund (ARFFX), Ariel International Fund (AINTX), and Ariel Global Fund (AGLOX). They also co-manage the Schwab Ariel Opportunities Fund ETF (SAEF).

Stake Increases:

First American Financial (FAF): FAF is a 2.56% of the portfolio stake established in 2011 at prices between ~$11 and ~$17. 2013 also saw a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$21 and ~$28. Recent activity follows: There was a ~10% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$61 and ~$70. The stock is now at $60.93. This quarter also saw a ~6% stake increase.

Charles River Laboratories (CRL): The 2.49% CRL stake saw a ~50% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$184 and ~$210. The next quarter saw a minor increase while during Q4 2023 there was marginal trimming. The stake was decreased by 11% in the next quarter at prices between ~$204 and ~$273. There was a ~20% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$179 and ~$221. The last quarter saw a ~30% further increase at prices between ~$92 and ~$154. The stock currently trades at ~$203. This quarter also saw a ~5% further increase.

Smucker J M (SJM): SJM is a 1.79% of the portfolio position established last year at prices between ~$106 and ~$135. There was a ~20% reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$93 and ~$121. The stock currently trades at ~$98. This quarter saw a marginal increase.

Algonquin Power (AQN), Axalta Coating (AXTA), Core Laboratories (CLB), Middleby (MIDD), and Prestige Consumer Healthcare (PBH): These positions were increased this quarter.

Note: They have significant ownership stakes in Core Laboratories and Prestige Consumer Healthcare.

Stake Decreases:

Sphere Entertainment (SPHR): The SPHR position came about as a result of the spin-out from MSG Entertainment. They had 6.66M shares of MSG Entertainment which now trades under this ticker. The net stake was reduced by ~20% during Q2 2023 and another ~15% in the next quarter. Q2 2024 saw a ~8% increase at prices between ~$33 and ~$49. That was followed by another similar increase during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$37 and ~$49. The next quarter also saw a ~10% increase at prices between ~$16 and ~$23. That was followed by a ~13% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$24 and ~$43. This quarter saw a ~16% reduction at prices between ~$38 and ~$63. The stock currently trades at ~$94.

Note: Regulatory filings since the quarter ended show them owning 4.1M shares (14.4% of business). This is compared to ~6M shares in the 13F Report.

Madison Square Garden Ent (MSGE): MSGE is the largest 13F stake at 3.74% of the portfolio. It is a very long-term stake. Recent activity follows. Q4 2024 saw a ~15% stake increase at prices between $33.05 and $43.75. The stock currently trades at $53.77. The last three quarters saw only minor adjustments.

Affiliated Managers Group (AMG): The 3.22% AMG long-term stake saw a ~9% increase during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$148 and ~$170. The stock is now at ~$292. The last few quarters saw minor trimming.

Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH): The 3.18% NCLH stake was built during the last two quarters at prices between ~$14 and ~$27. There was a minor ~2% trimming this quarter. The stock is now at $23.11.

Resideo Technologies (REZI): The ~3% REZI stake was built during the three quarters through Q2 2022 at prices between ~$19.50 and ~$28. There was a ~20% reduction during Q2 2024 at prices between $19.20 and $22.34. That was followed by another ~25% selling this quarter at prices between ~$22 and ~$43. The stock currently trades at $35.64.

Note: They have a ~5.4% ownership stake in the business.

Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL): The ~3% JLL position is a very long-term position first purchased in 2001. Next year saw a huge stake build-up at prices between ~$14.50 and ~$25. The position had seen selling since 2004. The bulk of the selling happened in 2006 at prices between ~$55 and ~$93. Recent activity follows. There was a ~8% trimming during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$173 and ~$212. The next quarter also saw a ~12% reduction at prices between ~$199 and ~$272. There was similar selling during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$247 and ~$284. The stock currently trades at ~$342. The last three quarters saw minor trimming.

Lazard Ltd. (LAZ): LAZ is a ~3% of the portfolio position first purchased in 2009 at prices between ~$19 and ~$38. The next year saw a stake-tripling at prices between ~$25 and ~$36. In 2014 there was a ~25% selling at prices between ~$39 and ~$50. Recent activity follows. The period since Q2 2024 has seen trimming most quarters. The stock currently trades at $49.27. They are harvesting gains.

OneSpaWorld Holdings (OSW): The ~3% OSW stake was built during the last two quarters at prices between ~$14 and ~$23. This quarter saw marginal trimming. The stock is now at $21.59.

Carlyle Group (CG): The 2.84% CG stake was built during the two quarters through Q3 2023 at prices between $27.50 and $42. The stake was decreased by 24% during Q1 2024 at prices between $37.83 and $47.23. That was followed by a ~10% trimming this quarter at prices between ~$51 and ~$70. The stock currently trades at $60.92.

Generac Holdings (GNRC): GNRC is a 2.63% stake that saw a ~27% increase during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$98 and ~$135. There was a ~15% increase during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$103 and ~$154. Q2 2024 saw a ~10% trimming at prices between ~$127 and ~$154. That was followed by a ~20% reduction during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$154 and ~$195. The next quarter saw a ~10% stake increase at prices between ~$123 and ~$165. There was a similar increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$100 and ~$144. This quarter saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$141 and ~$203. The stock is now at ~$140.

Envista Holdings (NVST): NVST is a 2.62% of the portfolio position. It was built during the last two years at prices between ~$15 and ~$36. The stock currently trades at $21.87. There was a ~3% trimming this quarter.

Mattel Inc. (MAT): MAT is currently at 2.48% of the portfolio. It was first purchased during 2016 at prices between ~$25 and ~$34. Next year saw a stake doubling at prices between ~$13 and ~$30.50. 2018 also saw a one-third stake increase at prices between ~$9.50 and ~$18. The stock currently trades at $19.88. The last several quarters saw only minor adjustments.

Mohawk Industries (MHK): MHK is a 2.34% of the portfolio position built over the three years through Q1 2022 at prices between ~$117 and ~$230. The stake was decreased by 13% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$98 and ~$131. The stock is now at ~$109. The last several quarters saw only minor adjustments.

Gentex Corp (GNTX): The 2.18% GNTX stake was purchased in Q3 2021 at prices between ~$27.50 and ~$38. Q3 2023 saw a ~18% selling at prices between ~$29 and ~$34. There was a ~11% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$23 and ~$29. The stock currently trades at $23.38. The last two quarters saw only minor adjustments.

ADT Inc. (ADT): ADT is currently at ~2% of the portfolio. The original stake was increased by ~30% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$6 and ~$7.50. The next quarter also saw a ~3% stake increase. There was a ~12% trimming in the three quarters through Q1 2025. That was followed by a similar reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$7.50 and ~$8.75. The stock currently trades at $8.06. This quarter also saw a ~5% trimming.

data.title: Carvana's Comeback: Structural Growth, S&P 500 Inclusion, But Pricey Valuation
data.description:
Carvana (CVNA) is up over 12,000% from its December 2022 lows, from $3.56 to $450 at the time of writing, marking one of the most remarkable turnarounds I've seen from near bankruptcy to now S&P 500 inclusion.

Before I dug into Carvana, I was quite sceptical. Usually, when you see a situation where a stock is up this much without much profitability, you immediately think it's a short. But in the case of Carvana, I came away with more of a neutral outlook, as I think the fundamentals are going to continue getting better, but the valuation is stretched. Perhaps the biggest takeaway for me is that the turnaround that happened is structural (permanent) rather than cyclical (temporary), so this could be the start of better fundamentals for many years to come.

In this write-up, I want to work through the P&L from revenue down to EBIT to make sense of why I think this is a structural change, then what the valuation looks like, plus the impact of S&P 500 inclusion.

Revenue

Q3 2025 revenue came in at $5.647 billion compared to the same quarter the year before, which was $3.655 billion; that's 54.1% YoY growth.

Source

I consider this an idiosyncratic change because this growth came in whilst brick-and-mortar competitors were seeing a decline, and the average sale price has remained stable to up slightly. So this rise in revenue is coming from more volume, all while US used car sales are growing in the low single digits, so it's a sign that there's true idiosyncratic growth in the Carvana brand.

As seen above, if we compare Carvana's current revenue to total US sales, they're only 1.5% of the market right now, so there's reasonable room for growth before any sort of TAM ceiling. Even though going from 1.5% to 4.5% in absolute terms isn't a lot, it's a large change in the economics, as many of the costs are fixed for Carvana, so the actual increase in operating income would be far more than 3x.

COGS

COGS at Carvana consists of four major components:

  • Vehicle acquisition cost (market-priced, largely uncontrollable)
  • Reconditioning costs
  • Logistics and transportation
  • Title, registration, and fulfillment expenses (technically an operating expense, but functionally acts more like per-unit COGS)

Only the vehicle acquisition cost is meaningfully cyclical. The other three are operational and therefore subject to structural improvement from the ADESA acquisition.

Prior to the acquisition, Carvana relied heavily on third-party reconditioning partners. This created three persistent economic problems that were structural disadvantages:

  1. Fragmented quality control, leading to inconsistent reconditioning outcomes and higher downstream costs
  2. Longer cycle times, which increased inventory days on hand and depreciation exposure
  3. Higher per-unit reconditioning costs, driven by third-party margins and inefficiencies

Post-integration, Carvana now controls the entire reconditioning workflow, from intake to inspection, parts sourcing, labour scheduling, and final retail readiness:

The wider margins reflect this change:

Logistics and transportation represent the second major operational COGS lever. Carvana’s logistics network has high fixed costs, including transport infrastructure, routing systems, and labor. As volumes increase within a fixed geographic footprint, the cost per unit declines through density. This is what occurred in 2024 and 2025 as unit volumes increased. In essence, there are economies of scale from the higher volume in a small geographic area, making for lower transportation costs.

Title, registration, and fulfillment costs have also benefited from scale and automation. Carvana has invested heavily in centralized systems that standardize these processes across jurisdictions. Once built, these systems scale sub-linearly with volume. In other words, it's a high fixed cost on the front-end, but that means a lower cost of revenue per vehicle as volume goes up. In the most recent investor presentation, it was highlighted as proprietary tech under the cost structure:

Current gross margins are sitting at 21.37%. Over time the improvements will naturally flatline as there are diminishing returns to how much the company can lower COGS, at which point, it becomes more of a volume game.

Source

Even if gross margins flatline here at around 20%, it's far better than traditional brick and mortar like CarMax (KMX):

Source

Even at its peak, the gross margins for CarMax were 17.6%. So as long as Carvana can keep gross margins above the traditional competition, it will have a price advantage.

Operating Expenses

When it comes to operating expenses, there's operating leverage in Carvana's model. Most operating expenses go to SG&A and don't grow as much as revenue does.

Source

As revenue grew 51.4% YoY, SG&A grew 26.86%.

The company defines SG&A (page 50) as the following:

SG&A expenses include expenses associated with advertising and providing customer service to customers, including financing, title and registration and limited warranty services, operating our vending machines, hubs, physical auctions, logistics and fulfillment network and other corporate overhead expenses, including expenses associated with information technology, product development, engineering, legal, accounting, finance, and business development. SG&A expenses exclude the costs of inspecting and reconditioning vehicles and transporting vehicles from the point of acquisition to the IRC, which are included in cost of sales, and payroll costs for our employees related to the development of software products for internal use, which are capitalized to software and depreciated over the estimated useful lives of the related assets.

We can go down the line of different operating expenses and whether they're fixed or scale with revenue.

The first is advertising, which is discretionary. As revenue grows, I don't expect that advertising will need to grow much, especially as customers start using Carvana as a default. This is semi-fixed.

The second is customer service functions: financing, title, and registration. These costs scale with volume but will become more efficient over time due to process standardisation and automation. I would consider this semi-variable, and as mentioned in the last section, even though it comes up as SG&A on the financials, it acts more like a cost of revenue.

The operation of vending machines, hubs, physical auctions, and the logistics and fulfillment network is often misunderstood as a variable expense. In reality, these assets introduce high fixed costs into SG&A. Vending machines, hubs, and auction facilities require staffing, maintenance, lease expense, and systems support regardless of throughput. Similarly, the logistics and fulfillment network carries fixed costs related to routing infrastructure, scheduling systems, and management oversight. As volume increases, these fixed costs are spread across more units, reducing SG&A per unit. This is why Carvana’s SG&A efficiency improved so rapidly once volumes recovered: the infrastructure was already in place, but underutilised during the downturn. So this is a clear fixed cost.

Corporate overhead—information technology, product development, engineering, legal, accounting, finance, and business development—is fixed.

All in all, I would say SG&A is semi-fixed, and as volumes continue to grow, it will grow at a slower rate. This is where it's pretty easy to see a large operating margin expansion due to operating leverage.

Valuation

Most investors likely think the valuation here is pricey, and I would definitely agree, but I would caution against thinking it's as overvalued as it first looks; nor would I recommend a short position.

The current run rate on EBIT annualises to around $2 billion with an EV of around $102 billion. So, at first glance, it seems clearly overvalued. But as mentioned, there's operating leverage as it grows, so it doesn't take a lot here to get a lower multiple. The aforementioned percentage of the US used car market held by Carvana is 1.5%. It really wouldn't take much to see a tripling to 4.5% in a few years if current revenue growth trends continue.

Say current gross margins stay stable, a 3x in annualised gross profit would be around $13 billion. Since we concluded SG&A is semi-fixed, it will grow, but around half of what the gross profit grows at, which has been the case over the last year. So that means a doubling in SG&A. That makes for an annualised EBIT of around $6.5 billion. This is a multiple against the EV of 15.7x, so still pricey, but once you do this back-of-the-napkin scenario, you can quickly understand how, if this level of growth continues to play out, the valuation might not be as overvalued as it first looks.

Overall, I feel Carvana is overvalued here, and I definitely wouldn't buy it, but at the same time, don't let the headline multiple trick you into thinking it's a clear short thesis.

Flows Over Fundamentals

In terms of what to pay attention to when putting on a trade, I always look at it in terms of the following importance:

  1. Flows
  2. Fundamentals
  3. Valuation

Flows are always at the top. For instance, a company can have great fundamentals, but if there's selling, especially if forced, then it's not a good time to get long and vice versa. A great example of flows over fundamentals is GameStop (GME) in 2021, when the fundamentals weren't great, but positioning was such that the float was more than 140% short, and there was a gamma squeeze to the upside.

As for valuation, I won't buy or sell purely on valuation. Far too many times have I seen companies that look like deep value become value traps, and similarly, high-quality and high-growth companies that trade for high multiples see further multiple expansion.

I bring these points up as Carvana will now be in the S&P 500 starting 22 December 2025, bringing in major flows. I'm writing this on the 20th, so it's likely that by the time you're reading this, you've seen what percentage of the S&P 500 its inclusion is. Based on comparing its float to the total S&P 500 float, I suspect it'll be around a 0.15% allocation.

S&P Global estimates that around $7 trillion in assets mechanically follow the S&P 500. So a 0.15% allocation is $10.5 billion. That's over 10% of the current market cap and 12% of the float, but since prices are set at the margin by short-term participants creating most of the volume, the price swing is multiples of that. Now it's true that there's been buying already in expectation of this event, which will likely sell the news, but nonetheless, this is an upside risk to be aware of.

I mainly write this section to say that although Carvana is likely overvalued, both the flows and better fundamentals aren't bearish, so I wouldn't bet against it.

Investor Takeaways

  • Carvana has had a true structural shift to profitability.
  • As revenue grows, operating leverage will multiply EBIT, and Carvana is at the inflection point for that. Hence, headline multiples are misleading.
  • Valuation is definitely stretched, but perhaps not by as much as it initially seems.
  • S&P 500 inclusion means price-insensitive buying, so I would avoid shorting here.
  • I've put a holding rating here as the underlying company fundamentals are positive, but the valuation here is far too pricey to justify at current fundamentals. It's possible in 2026 we see a significant pullback, at which point Carvana will be on my watch list for a potential long then.
